steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Khudadad concluded that the Cairo site is inconsistent with the partition hypothesis. The partition hypothesis claims two distinctly different biomes existed — one with Eospermatopteris, one with Archaeopteris. To undermine that, we need evidence that the two plants coexisted at the same site during the Devonian.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• Choice A — Although the site appears to have been a tropical deltaic forest during the Devonian period, fossil evidence suggests that *Eospermatopteris* was present at that time.: Eospermatopteris is the expected plant of a tropical deltaic forest — its presence there actually supports the hypothesis, not undermines it. ✗
  • Choice B — The site appears to have been a tropical deltaic forest early in the Devonian period but transitioned to an arid floodplain forest after the end of the Devonian period.: The transition happens after the Devonian, so this doesn't address what both plants were doing during the Devonian. ✗
  • Choice C — The site appears to have been an arid floodplain forest during the Devonian period and contains fossil evidence of contemporaneous *Eospermatopteris* and *Archaeopteris*.: An arid floodplain forest (home to Archaeopteris) also contained Eospermatopteris at the same time — the two plants coexisted, directly contradicting the 'distinctly different' biomes claim. ✓
  • Choice D — Fossil evidence from the site suggests that several forest plant species other than *Eospermatopteris* and *Archaeopteris* lived there during the Devonian period.: Other species being present says nothing about whether the two key biome-defining plants coexisted. ✗
C is correct.
